steps.text: Verify that there are no significant vacuum-side leaks using the low-flow test.
Verify that there are no significant vacuum-side leaks using the vacuum-decay test.
Verify the gas cell pathlength according to the procedures in revised section 10.6.4.
Follow the QA spike procedure assuming that the method has been validated for each of the target analyte at the source.
Perform a stratification check as part of the revised sampling procedures.
Compare the pre- and post-test CTS spectra.
Follow the revised recording and reporting requirements.
Incorporate the requirements from section 8.4 for digital data storage.
Determine the optimum sampling system configuration for measuring the target analytes.
Based on the source gas characteristics, select the equipment for extracting and transporting gas samples.
Select the techniques and/or equipment for the measurement of sample pressures and temperatures in the sample cell.
Heat sample transport lines to maintain sample temperature at least 10 °F (5 °C) above the dew point for all sample constituents.
Select a spectroscopic configuration for the application.
Prepare an analysis algorithm for acquired spectra.
Correct for differences between the reference pathlength (LR), temperature (TR), and pressure (PR), and the actual conditions used for collecting the sample spectra.
Choose a mathematical technique for analyzing spectral data by comparison with reference spectra.
Use a sufficient number (>3) of reference spectra in the bracketed range to demonstrate linearity in that concentration range.
Select specific wavelengths in each region where the target analyte does not overlap with an interfering compound.
Conduct the leak check by following the procedures specified in section 11.1.
Verify detector linearity by following the procedures specified in section 11.2.
Follow the data storage requirements specified in section 11.8.
Flow dry nitrogen through the gas cell and verify that no significant amounts of absorbing species are present.
Collect a background spectrum using a signal averaging period equal to or longer than that being used for averaging of source sample spectra.
Flow the CTS gas through the cell and verify that the measured concentration is stable to within the uncertainty of the gas standard.
Conduct a QA spike per the instructions in section 11.4.
While sampling, monitor the signal transmittance.
After the sampling run, record another CTS spectrum.
Perform post-run QA per section 9.1.2 of this method.
Verify that the sample integration time is sufficient to achieve the required signal-to-noise ratio.
Assign a unique file name to each spectrum.
Inspect the sample spectra immediately after the run to verify the gas matrix composition was close to the expected matrix composition.
Verify that the sampling and instrumental parameters were appropriate for the actual stack conditions.
Compare the pre- and post-test CTS spectra. The peak absorbance in the pre- and post-test CTS must be ±5% of the mean value.
Perform the analyte spike procedure to evaluate the performance of the sampling system.

Conduct the spike procedure consisting of measuring the source emissions concentration, addition of reference gas, and measurement of the resulting standard addition elevated source gas concentration.
Ensure each dynamic spike or SA replicate consists of a measurement of the source emissions concentration with and without the addition of the species of interest.
Introduce the SA gases in such a manner that the entire sampling system is challenged.
Perform pre and post-test spiking consisting of at least 3 replicates.
Determine the absolute noise equivalent absorption (NEA) for an analytical region by flowing nitrogen or zero air through the gas sample cell.
Collect two background spectra in succession while the nitrogen or zero air is continuously flowing through the cell.
Calculate NEARMSCTS per the provided equation.
Obtain reference spectra for each analyte, interferant, surrogate, CTS, and tracer.

Report traceability and other pertinent information for each reference spectrum, including: temperature, pressure, concentration, cylinder source and specifications, spectral regions of analysis used for quantitation, and calibration fit equations and correlations.
Ensure that the FTIR spectral resolution and line position, cell pathlength, temperature and pressure, and apodization function are known and reported.
Verify that reference spectra for each target compound must bracket the concentration of that compound in the sample stream.
Directly inject the compound of interest into the FTIR at a concentration lower than that found in the sample stream and within three times the method detection level, if needed.
Wait for the concentration of the compound to stabilize.
Verify that the concentration as determined via the calibration curve is within 10% of the cylinder value or else do not proceed with testing.
Use calculated spectra from HITRAN or PNNL at the lower end of the bracketing range, if needed.
Collect reference spectra prior to testing if necessary.
Record a set of CTS spectra.
Collect a set of the reference spectra at two or more concentrations in triplicate over the desired concentration range.
Ensure the top of the concentration range is less than 10 times that of the bottom of the range.
Collect a second set of CTS spectra.
Ensure the maximum accepted concentration for each compound shall be higher than the maximum estimated.
Permanently store the background and interferograms digitally, and separately.
Document details of the mathematical process (i.e., apodization function) for generating the spectra from these interferograms.
Record sample pressure (Pr), sample temperature (Tr), reference absorption pathlength (Lr), and interferogram signal integration period (tsr).
Flow the CTS through the FTIR cell.
Record a set of the absorption spectra of the CTS, and record the temperature, pressure, and concentration of the CTS.
Record the instrument manufacturer’s nominal absorption pathlength, nominal spectral resolution, and the CTS signal integration period.
Calculate the reference cell absorption pathlength, according to the following equation: Lr = reference cell absorption pathlength.
Flow ambient air through the gas cell.
Verify the instrument resolution using a water absorbance peak near either 1,918 cm¥1, 3,050 cm¥1, or 3,920 cm¥1.
Mix additional humified air or nitrogen with the ambient flow, to achieve this absorbance.
Record an absorbance spectrum and measure the FWHH of the chosen water peak.
Verify that there are no significant vacuum-side leaks using one of the leak tests described in this section.
Perform the vacuum-side leak check after each installation at the sampling or measurement location.
A leak may be detected either by measuring a small amount of flow when there should be zero flow, or by measuring the vacuum decay rate.
To test for leaks using loss of vacuum you must know the vacuum-side volume of your sampling system to within ±10% of its true volume.
Seal the probe end of the system by capping or plugging the end of the sample probe.
Start sampling pumps and operate them until the pressure stabilizes.
Observe/measure the flow through the vacuum-side of the sampling system. A flow of less than 0.5% of the system’s normal in-use flow rate is acceptable.
Seal the probe end of the system as close to the probe opening as possible by capping or plugging the end of the sample probe.
Operate all vacuum pumps. Draw a vacuum on the sampling system and let the pressure on the system stabilize.

Turn off the sample pumps and seal the system under a vacuum of 250 mmHg greater than the source static pressure. Record the absolute pressure and the system absolute temperature every 30 seconds for 5 minutes. The leak rate must be equal to or less than 2.5 mmHg per minute.

Observe the single beam instrument response in the frequency region below the detector cutoff (usually <400 cm¥1), where the detector response is known to be zero. Verify that the detector response is 'flat' and equal to zero in this region, or at least 100 times less than the peak signal in the entire spectrum.
If the response is not linear, decrease the aperture or attenuate the IR beam, and repeat the linearity check until the detector response is linear.
Verify the gas cell pathlength of your instrument by following the procedure found in section 10.6.4 of this method.
Choose one of two options and perform the standard addition procedure listed in section 9.3 of this method.
Use a certified standard (±2% accuracy) for an analyte that has been validated at the source. One may either spike each analyte of interest or choose an appropriate surrogate.
An appropriate surrogate must have a vapor pressure that is less than or equal to the analyte of interest and be less soluble in water.
The wavelength at which the surrogate is to be quantified must be reported and be within 100 wavenumbers of a wavenumber that will be used to quantify the analyte of interest.
Ensure the pKa of a surrogate is within 20% of the pKa of the analyte of interest.
Proceed with testing if the spike recovery is within 70–130%.
Use a non-certified cylinder for an analyte that has been validated at the source.
Proceed with testing if the spike recovery is within 90–110%.
Perform sampling using a continuous flow of source gas.
Perform a stratification check to justify sampling at a single location during testing.
Use a probe of appropriate length to measure the analyte of interest at each of 12 traverse points.
Calculate the mean measured concentration for all sampling points.
Calculate the percent stratification of each traverse point.
Perform testing at a single point if the gas stream is considered unstratified.
Sample using the 3-point short line if the gas stream is considered minimally stratified.
Sample using the stratification check procedure if the gas stream is considered stratified.
Assign a unique filename to each spectrum and each corresponding interferogram.
Measure the temperature of the gas cell directly.
Calibrate the temperature measurement device to within ±0.1 °C every 12 months.
Measure the gas cell pressure empirically.
Calibrate the measurement device for pressure to within ±1 mmHg every 12 months.

preconditions.text: If the transmittance (relative to background) changes by 5% or more in any analytical spectral region, obtain a new background spectrum.

constraints.text: Select techniques to ensure gas sample transport lines and system components must be heated sufficiently through their entire length to transport target compounds to the IR sample cell.
